text: I changed a rollout from ten percent to twenty. The scary part? An agent can confidently bring back the old answer. In production, stale truth is worse than no memory.
delivery: Begin like you are telling a colleague about a real incident. Take a full beat after the first sentence and after the question. Land 'stale truth' quietly, without drama.

text: So I built DREAM. Qwen understands what experience means; deterministic lifecycle rules decide what can come back.
delivery: Sound personally invested and clear. Give DREAM and Qwen light emphasis, then relax through the second clause.

text: This is the public build. It is running in Singapore, on Alibaba Function Compute.
delivery: Make this casual and concrete, like turning your laptop toward a colleague. Speak slowly, around 125 words per minute, with a clear pause between the sentences.

text: First, I set the canary at ten percent, for thirty minutes. Qwen recognizes the preference. It gives me an inspectable receipt.
delivery: Use an easy live-demo cadence at about 130 words per minute. Pause between all three sentences. Let the numbers sound natural rather than announced.

text: Then I change it to twenty percent for forty-five minutes. Qwen catches the conflict; DREAM keeps only the new value active.
delivery: Sound lightly curious on the change, then quietly satisfied when the conflict is resolved. Use a short pause after 'conflict'.

text: In a fresh session, DREAM returns twenty percent in nineteen of sixty-four tokens. The old instruction never enters context.
delivery: Keep this concise and matter-of-fact. Put gentle emphasis on 'fresh session' and 'never'.

text: The test I cared about was persistence. I saved a Qwen-curated memory, rebuilt the same source, and forced a new Function Compute instance. The instance ID changed; the memory, decision, and Qwen receipt did not. Twenty simultaneous conflicting writes also succeeded, leaving one active truth and nineteen historical versions.
delivery: This is the emotional proof point. Start personally, leave a real pause after 'persistence', then sound genuinely relieved at 'did not'. Keep the final concurrency result grounded, not triumphant.

text: In a clearly labeled synthetic evaluation, the same Qwen model improved from 25.3 to 48.7 across seven paired cases. A separate twenty-four-case lifecycle suite passed every recall, stale-leak, and token-budget check.
delivery: Use a transparent, analytical tone. Clearly acknowledge 'synthetic' and avoid sales-style emphasis on the scores.

text: Underneath: Angular and FastAPI on Function Compute; Qwen Cloud for meaning; Tablestore transactions for one current truth; and temporary credentials from a narrow RAM role.
delivery: Explain this like a senior engineer sketching the stack for a peer: fluent, compact, and unforced.

text: I do not want longer history. I want one current, reviewable truth, for the next decision.
delivery: Speak slowly, around 120 words per minute. Keep the ending personal, warm, and understated, with a full clean pause between the two sentences.
